'''Kirk Yeaman''' (born 15 September 1983) is a former English professional [[rugby league]] footballer who played as a {{rlp|ce}}. An [[England national rugby league team|England]] and [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain]] international, he played his entire club career for [[Hull FC]] in the [[Super League]], but also made two appearances for [[Doncaster R.L.F.C.|Doncaster]] as a dual-registration player.

He made 373 appearances for Hull FC between 2001 and 2016 before retiring after a 15-year career with the club, but briefly came out of retirement in May 2018 to register himself as a player due to the number of injury problems the club had.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.rugby-league.com/article/52434/yeaman-returns-from-retirement-for-hull-fc|title = Yeaman returns from retirement for Hull FC}}</ref>

==Early life==
Yeaman was born at Hedon Road Maternity Hospital in [[Kingston upon Hull|Hull]] on 15 September 1983. He is the son of Debbie, who worked for Hull-based Smith & Nephew, and Dale Yeaman, a laminator. He has an older brother, Scott, who played [[rugby league]] for [[Hull Kingston Rovers]] and [[York Wasps]], and a younger sister, Jessica.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Yeaman |first1=Kirk |last2=Bond |first2=David |year=2017 |title=Yeamo: My Career in Black and White |publisher=Vertical Editions |isbn=978-1-908847-06-5}}</ref> He attended [[Bransholme High School]],<ref>{{cite news |title=Hull's home-grown dream team of 13 rugby league stars |url=https://www.pressreader.com/uk/hull-daily-mail/20200317/281676846984015 |work=Hull Daily Mail |date=17 March 2020 |via=[[PressReader]]}}</ref> and played junior rugby league for [[Myton Warriors]].<ref>{{cite web |title=Community Club of the Week: Myton Warriors |url=https://www.hullfc.com/blog/2022/03/19/community-club-of-the-week-myton-warriors/ |website=Hull FC |access-date=7 September 2023}}</ref>

== Career ==
Yeaman came up through the ranks at Hull, a centre who was likened to [[Jamie Lyon]]<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/sport/rugbyleague/2349455/Yeamans-daunting-debut.html|title=Yeaman's daunting debut|author=David Burke|date=2 November 2006|work=The Telegraph|publisher=Telegraph Media Group Limited|access-date=10 August 2011}}</ref>  and also a proven try scorer. He made his debut in August 2001 against [[London Broncos]].<ref>{{cite web |title=King reigns over London |url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2001/aug/20/rugbyleague.hullfcrl |website=The Guardian |access-date=30 July 2023 |date=20 August 2001}}</ref> He scored 17 tries at the end of the 2005 season and an impressive tally in 2006 that took him over the 20 try mark.

Hull F.C. reached the [[2006 Super League Grand final]] to be contested against [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]], and Yeaman played at centre in his side's 4-26 loss.<ref>{{cite news|title=St Helens 26-4 Hull FC|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/super_league/6035553.stm|access-date=18 August 2013|newspaper=[[BBC News]]|date=14 October 2006}}</ref> His performances in 2006 saw him included in the Super League Dream Team.

Despite some injuries, he was called up to the [[2006 Rugby League Tri-Nations|Tri Nations]] in Australia, playing in two matches.

Yeaman signed a three-year contract extension with Hull [http://www.hullfc.com/index.php?/news/more/yeaman-signs/] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070927222918/http://www.hullfc.com/index.php?/news/more/yeaman-signs/ |date=27 September 2007 }}, and he remained with the club until the end of the 2010 season.

He was forced to rule himself out contention for the [[2008 Rugby League World Cup squads#England|England training squad]] for the [[2008 Rugby League World Cup]] through injury.<ref name="Yeaman to face surgery decision">{{cite web|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/super_league/hull/7629732.stm|title=Yeaman to face surgery decision|publisher=BBC|date=2008-09-22|access-date=2008-10-03}}</ref>

Yeaman has a [[2005 Challenge Cup]] winners medal after Hull beat Leeds 25-24 in the first ever August Challenge Cup Final, he was also a member of the Hull squad that reached the 2006 Super League Grand Final (losing to St. Helens) and was a double try scorer In Hull's 28-16 defeat also against St. Helens in the 2008 Challenge Cup Final at [[Wembley Stadium]].

In 2011, Yeaman was rewarded for his form with Hull F.C. by being called up to the England Squad along with wing partner Tom Briscoe for the Four Nations. He was also included in the Super League Dream Team, again along with Briscoe.

At the start of the 2016 season, Yeaman made two appearances for [[Doncaster R.L.F.C.|Doncaster]] as a dual-registration player.<ref>{{cite web |title='It has been tough on the sidelines', says Hull FC's Kirk Yeaman |url=https://www.yorkshirepost.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/it-has-been-tough-sidelines-says-hull-fcs-kirk-yeaman-1801551 |website=Yorkshire Post |access-date=18 June 2022 |date=21 March 2016}}</ref>

On 3 June 2016, a try against Widnes took Yeaman over the 700 point mark for Hull, consisting of 177 tries. Yeaman is only the second player in Hull's history to score 700 points without kicking a single goal. The late [[Clive Sullivan]] MBE is the other.

He played in the [[2016 Challenge Cup Final]] victory over the [[Warrington Wolves]] at [[Wembley Stadium]].<ref name="Hull FC’s Jamie Shaul’s late try takes Challenge Cup away from Warrington">{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2016/aug/27/hull-warrington-challenge-cup-final-match-report|title=Hull FC's Jamie Shaul's late try takes Challenge Cup away from Warrington|publisher=Guardian|date=27 August 2016|access-date=7 November 2019}}</ref><ref name="Challenge Cup final: Hull FC 12-10 Warrington Wolves">{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/37169016|title=Challenge Cup final: Hull FC 12-10 Warrington Wolves|publisher=BBC|date=27 August 2016|access-date=7 November 2019}}</ref>

Yeaman announced his retirement as a player at the end of the 2016 season.<ref>{{cite web |title=Kirk Yeaman: Hull FC favourite to retire at end of 2016 season |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/37444758 |website=BBC Sport |access-date=18 June 2022 |date=22 September 2016}}</ref> He is third on Hull's all-time try scoring list with 177; only Clive Sullivan and Ivor Watts are ahead of him.

After retiring as a player, Yeaman joined the coaching staff at Hull. In 2018, Yeaman came out of retirement to play against local rivals [[Hull Kingston Rovers|Hull KR]] in a 34–22 win at the [[Magic Weekend]].<ref>{{cite web |title=Hull KR 22-34 Hull FC: Black and Whites take Magic Weekend derby honours |url=https://www.skysports.com/rugby-league/hull-k-r-vs-hull/48361 |website=Sky Sports |access-date=18 June 2022 |date=21 May 2018}}</ref> He left his coaching role in 2021 to start a career with the [[His Majesty's Prison Service|Prison Service]].<ref>{{cite web |title=Kirk Yeaman leaves Hull FC coaching position in favour of new career path |url=https://www.hulldailymail.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/kirk-yeaman-leaves-hull-fc-5852840 |website=Hull Daily Mail |access-date=18 June 2022 |date=31 August 2021}}</ref>

In 2023, he was inducted into Hull FC's Hall of Fame.<ref>{{cite web |title=Hull FC add four club legends to Hall of Fame at special ceremony |url=https://www.yorkshirepost.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/hull-fc-add-four-club-legends-to-hall-of-fame-at-special-ceremony-4001249 |website=Yorkshire Post |access-date=30 July 2023 |date=25 January 2023}}</ref>
